chapters.chapter: Orienting Yourself: The Use of Coordinates

topics.topic: The Need for a Coordinate System
topics.meaning: A coordinate system is a structured framework that uses numbers to describe the exact location of points or objects.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A system of coordinates is a structured framework, like the grid lines on a map or graph paper, that lets us use numbers to describe exact physical locations. The idea of grid-based thinking has deep roots in Bharat: city streets of the Sindhu-Sarasvati Civilisation ran in North-South and East-West directions at uniform distances of about 10 metres, forming a coordinate system in practice. Baudhayana used East-West and North-South lines for geometric constructions. Brahmagupta formalised zero and negative numbers, which are essential for the origin and negative axes of the modern Cartesian plane. Rene Descartes (1637 CE) finally formalised that any point in a plane can be described by two numbers, the distances from two perpendicular axes, bringing geometry and algebra together.
topics.easyExplanation: A coordinate system is just a clever way of using numbers to say exactly where something is, like saying a shop is 3 streets east and 2 streets north of the centre.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify the need
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We need a way to describe where a point lies, not just on a line but anywhere on a flat surface.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use a grid
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two perpendicular reference lines and equal units let us count distances in two directions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Locate the point
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Every point gets two numbers, telling how far across and how far up or down it lies.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect to history
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ancient cities and mathematicians like Brahmagupta and Descartes developed exactly these ideas.

examples: A merchant in an ancient grid city finds a warehouse by counting 4 streets East and 3 streets North from the centre, giving location (4, 3).
On graph paper, the point 'two right and one up' from the centre is written (2, 1).

importantKeywords.definition: A framework using numbers to fix the location of points in space.
The fixed central point from which all distances are measured.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The city centre where the two main roads cross.

importantKeywords.definition: A network of evenly spaced perpendicular lines used to locate points.

topics.quickSummary: A coordinate system uses numbers and perpendicular reference lines to describe exact positions, an idea with deep roots from ancient grid cities to Descartes.
revisionNotes: Coordinates describe exact locations using numbers.
Grid-based thinking has ancient roots in Bharat (Sindhu-Sarasvati cities).
Brahmagupta's zero and negative numbers made the modern plane possible.
Descartes (1637) formalised describing points by two numbers.

topics.topic: Axes, Origin and the x- and y-coordinates
topics.meaning: The x-axis and y-axis are two perpendicular number lines whose point of intersection, the origin, has coordinates (0,0).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The two-dimensional coordinate system uses two lines at right angles. The horizontal line is the x-axis and the vertical line is the y-axis. Their point of intersection is the origin O with coordinates (0, 0). Distances from O are marked in equal units on both axes; distances to the right or upward are positive, to the left or downward are negative. For a point P(x, y), x is the perpendicular distance from the y-axis measured along the x-axis, and y is the perpendicular distance from the x-axis measured along the y-axis. A point of the form (x, 0) lies on the x-axis and a point of the form (0, y) lies on the y-axis.
topics.easyExplanation: Think of two number lines crossing at zero: one lying flat (x) and one standing up (y). The crossing point is the origin (0,0), and every point is named by how far it sits along each line.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Draw the axes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Draw a horizontal x-axis and a vertical y-axis crossing at the origin O.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mark equal units
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Mark equal units; right and up are positive, left and down are negative.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read x then y
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The first number is the distance along the x-axis, the second along the y-axis.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Special points
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Points on the x-axis look like (x,0); points on the y-axis look like (0,y); the origin is (0,0).

examples: Point B is 4.5 units right of O on the x-axis, so B = (4.5, 0).
Point H is 4 units above O on the y-axis, so H = (0, 4).

importantKeywords.definition: The horizontal reference line of the coordinate plane.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The flat line where points like (3,0) sit.

importantKeywords.definition: The vertical reference line of the coordinate plane.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The upright line where points like (0,4) sit.

importantKeywords.definition: The ordered pair (x, y) giving a point's position.

topics.quickSummary: Two perpendicular axes meet at the origin (0,0); a point P(x,y) is x units along the x-axis and y units along the y-axis.
revisionNotes: x-axis is horizontal, y-axis is vertical, they meet at origin (0,0).
Right/up are positive; left/down are negative.
x = distance from y-axis; y = distance from x-axis.
Points on x-axis are (x,0); on y-axis are (0,y).

topics.meaning: The two axes divide the Cartesian plane into four regions called quadrants, each with a characteristic sign pattern for its coordinates.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The plane containing the axes is called the Cartesian plane, coordinate plane or xy-plane. The axes divide it into four parts called quadrants, numbered anticlockwise. Quadrant I has both x and y positive; Quadrant II has x negative and y positive; Quadrant III has both negative; Quadrant IV has x positive and y negative. For example, S(3, -5) lies in Quadrant IV and Q(-5, 3) lies in Quadrant II. Knowing the signs of the coordinates lets us immediately tell which quadrant a point lies in.
topics.easyExplanation: The crossing axes cut the page into four corners. Just by looking at whether each number is plus or minus, you can say which corner the point is in.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Look at the signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Check whether the x-coordinate and y-coordinate are positive or negative.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Quadrant I and II
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: (+,+) is Quadrant I; (-,+) is Quadrant II.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Quadrant III and IV
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: (-,-) is Quadrant III; (+,-) is Quadrant IV.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Points on axes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Points with a zero coordinate lie on an axis, not in any quadrant.

examples: S(3, -5): x is positive, y is negative, so S is in Quadrant IV.
Q(-5, 3): x is negative, y is positive, so Q is in Quadrant II.

importantKeywords.definition: One of the four regions into which the axes divide the plane.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Quadrant I is the top-right region.

importantKeywords.definition: The flat plane containing the x- and y-axes.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The full sheet of graph paper.

importantKeywords.definition: The pair of plus/minus signs that fixes the quadrant.

topics.quickSummary: The axes split the plane into four quadrants with sign patterns (+,+), (-,+), (-,-), (+,-) for I, II, III, IV.
revisionNotes: Quadrant I: (+,+); Quadrant II: (-,+); Quadrant III: (-,-); Quadrant IV: (+,-).
Quadrants are numbered anticlockwise from top-right.
Points with a zero coordinate lie on an axis.
Signs of (x,y) immediately reveal the quadrant.

topics.topic: Distance Between Points on or Parallel to an Axis
topics.meaning: When two points lie on the same horizontal or vertical line, the distance between them is the absolute difference of the changing coordinate.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can easily find the distance between two points when the segment joining them is parallel to an axis. For two points (x1, y) and (x2, y) on a horizontal line, the distance is the absolute value |x2 - x1| of the difference of the x-coordinates. For two points (x, y1) and (x, y2) on a vertical line, the distance is |y2 - y1|. This works because along such a segment only one coordinate changes, so we simply measure the shift along that single axis. These simple distances are the building blocks for the general distance formula.
topics.easyExplanation: If two dots are on the same flat line, just subtract their across-numbers (and take the positive value). If they are on the same upright line, subtract their up-numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check the alignment
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: See whether the points share the same y (horizontal) or same x (vertical).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Subtract the changing coordinate
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For a horizontal segment subtract the x-values; for vertical subtract the y-values.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Take the absolute value
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Distance is always non-negative, so use |x2 - x1| or |y2 - y1|.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State the distance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write the result in units.

examples: Distance between (2, 5) and (7, 5): |7 - 2| = 5 units.
Distance between (3, -1) and (3, 4): |4 - (-1)| = 5 units.

importantKeywords.definition: The non-negative size of a number, ignoring its sign.
A line segment where the y-coordinate stays the same.
A line segment where the x-coordinate stays the same.

topics.quickSummary: Distance along a horizontal line is |x2 - x1| and along a vertical line is |y2 - y1|.
revisionNotes: Horizontal distance = |x2 - x1|.
Vertical distance = |y2 - y1|.
Only one coordinate changes along such segments.
These are the basis of the general distance formula.

topics.meaning: The distance between any two points (x1,y1) and (x2,y2) is sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2), found using the Baudhayana-Pythagoras theorem.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When the segment joining two points is not parallel to either axis, we use the Baudhayana-Pythagoras theorem. We drop a horizontal and a vertical segment so the two points become the ends of the hypotenuse of a right triangle. The horizontal shift equals the difference of the x-coordinates and the vertical shift equals the difference of the y-coordinates. By the theorem, the distance between (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2). It makes no difference whether the differences are positive or negative because we square them, so reflection of a figure preserves all side lengths.
topics.easyExplanation: Make a right triangle: the across-gap and the up-gap are the two short sides, and the straight-line distance is the slanted side. Use the Pythagoras rule to get it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the two gaps
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Compute the across difference (x2 - x1) and the up difference (y2 - y1).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Square each gap
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Square both differences so signs do not matter.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the two squared gaps together.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Take the square root
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The distance is sqrt of that sum, in units.

examples: Distance from A(3,4) to D(7,1): sqrt((7-3)^2 + (1-4)^2) = sqrt(16+9) = sqrt(25) = 5 units.
Distance from D(7,1) to M(9,6): sqrt((9-7)^2 + (6-1)^2) = sqrt(4+25) = sqrt(29) units.

importantKeywords.definition: sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2), the distance between two points.
In a right triangle the square on the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares on the other two sides.
The longest side of a right triangle, opposite the right angle.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The slanted segment joining two points.

topics.quickSummary: The distance between (x1,y1) and (x2,y2) is sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2) by the Baudhayana-Pythagoras theorem.
revisionNotes: Use sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2) for any two points.
It comes from making a right triangle.
Squaring removes the effect of signs.
Reflections preserve distances.

topics.meaning: Reflecting a figure across an axis changes the signs of coordinates but keeps all distances the same.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a triangle is reflected in the y-axis, each point's x-coordinate changes sign while the y-coordinate stays the same; for example A(3,4) becomes A'(-3,4). When reflected in the x-axis, the y-coordinate changes sign. Computing the side lengths of the reflected triangle using the distance formula gives exactly the same values as the original, because the horizontal and vertical shifts have the same size. Thus reflection preserves the lengths of all sides; lengths are unchanged even though positions and signs change.
topics.easyExplanation: Flipping a shape across a line is like looking at it in a mirror. The shape may face the other way and the number signs flip, but its size and side lengths stay exactly the same.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choose the mirror line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Decide whether you reflect across the y-axis or the x-axis.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Change the right sign
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reflecting in the y-axis flips the sign of x; reflecting in the x-axis flips the sign of y.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find new coordinates
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write the reflected coordinates of each vertex.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check distances
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use the distance formula to confirm side lengths are unchanged.

examples: Reflecting A(3,4) in the y-axis gives A'(-3,4); the distance to D'(-7,1) is still sqrt(16+9) = 5 units.
Reflecting (2,5) in the x-axis gives (2,-5).

importantKeywords.definition: A flip of a figure across a line that produces a mirror image.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A(3,4) becomes A'(-3,4) across the y-axis.

importantKeywords.definition: The line across which a figure is reflected.
A distance that stays the same under a transformation.

topics.quickSummary: Reflecting across an axis flips one coordinate's sign but keeps every distance unchanged.
revisionNotes: Reflection in y-axis flips sign of x; in x-axis flips sign of y.
Reflected figures are mirror images.
All side lengths are preserved.
Distance formula confirms lengths are equal.

topics.meaning: The midpoint of a segment is the point exactly halfway between its endpoints, found by averaging the coordinates.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When M is the midpoint of segment ST, its coordinates are the averages of the coordinates of S and T. If S = (x1, y1) and T = (x2, y2), then M = ((x1 + x2)/2, (y1 + y2)/2). This connection lets us check whether a given point is a midpoint and lets us find a missing endpoint when one endpoint and the midpoint are known. The same averaging idea extends to points of trisection by dividing the segment into equal parts.
topics.easyExplanation: The middle point is found by taking the average of the two end x-numbers and the average of the two end y-numbers, just like finding a number halfway between two numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the endpoints
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note the coordinates of the two endpoints S and T.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Average the x-values
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the two x-coordinates and divide by 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Average the y-values
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the two y-coordinates and divide by 2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the midpoint
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine the two averages as (x, y).

examples: If M(-7,1) is the midpoint of A(3,-4) and B(x,y), then (3+x)/2 = -7 gives x = -17, and (-4+y)/2 = 1 gives y = 6, so B = (-17, 6).

importantKeywords.definition: The point exactly halfway between two endpoints.
The sum of values divided by how many there are.
The two points that divide a segment into three equal parts.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Points one-third and two-thirds along a segment.

topics.quickSummary: The midpoint of a segment is ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2), the average of the endpoint coordinates.
revisionNotes: Midpoint = average of x's and average of y's.
Use it to find a missing endpoint.
Trisection extends the averaging idea.
Halfway point connects to the distance formula.

topics.topic: Checking Collinearity and Points on a Circle
topics.meaning: Using distances, we can test whether three points are collinear and whether a point lies inside, on, or outside a circle.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Distances let us test geometric properties without plotting. Three points are collinear (on one straight line) if the distance between the two outer points equals the sum of the distances of an intermediate point to each of them; otherwise they form a triangle. To test a circle centred at the origin, we compute the distance of each point from the centre: if it equals the radius the point lies on the circle, if it is less the point lies inside, and if it is greater the point lies outside. These ideas apply to problems such as checking whether icons overlap on a screen.
topics.easyExplanation: By measuring distances you can tell if three dots lie in a straight line, and whether a dot sits exactly on a circle, inside it, or outside it, just by comparing the distance to the circle's radius.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compute distances
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the distances between the relevant points using the distance formula.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test collinearity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Three points are in a line if one distance equals the sum of the other two.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare with radius
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For a circle, compare each point's distance from the centre with the radius.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Classify the point
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Equal means on the circle, smaller means inside, larger means outside.

examples: For M(-3,-4), A(0,0), G(6,8): OA = 5, OG = 10, MG = 15 and MA + AG... checking shows MA = 5, AG = 10, MG = 15 so MA + AG = MG, the points are collinear.
A point at distance 9 from the centre of a circle of radius 9 lies on the circle; distance 7 lies inside; distance 12 lies outside.

importantKeywords.definition: Lying on a single straight line.
The fixed distance from a circle's centre to any point on it.
Whether a point's distance from the centre equals, is less than, or exceeds the radius.

topics.quickSummary: Distances reveal whether points are collinear and whether a point lies on, inside, or outside a circle.
revisionNotes: Collinear if one distance equals the sum of the other two.
Compare distance from centre with radius to place a point.
Equal = on, less = inside, greater = outside.
No plotting is needed, only calculation.

topics.meaning: An algebraic expression combines numbers, variables and operation symbols; its parts are called terms, with coefficients and constants.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic expression combines numbers, variables and operation symbols. For example, if Raju buys x red boxes of 4 pens each and y blue boxes of 5 pencils each, plus 3 free pens, the total is the expression 4x + 5y + 3. Here 4x, 5y and 3 are terms; x and y are variables (also called letter-numbers); 4 and 5 are the coefficients of x and y; and 3 is a constant. Expressions can involve one variable, like x^2 + 5x + 1, or several variables, like 200l + 160w + 50lw. Recognising terms, variables, coefficients and constants is the first step in working with polynomials.
topics.easyExplanation: An algebraic expression is a math phrase made of numbers and letters joined by plus, minus or times. Each piece separated by + or - is a term; the number multiplying a letter is its coefficient.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Spot the variables
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Identify the letters that stand for unknown numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Break into terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Separate the expression at the + and - signs.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read the coefficients
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The number multiplying each variable is its coefficient.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the constant
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A term with no variable is the constant.

examples: In 4x + 5y + 3: terms are 4x, 5y, 3; variables are x and y; coefficients are 4 and 5; constant is 3.
In x^2 + 5x + 1: the coefficient of x^2 is 1, the coefficient of x is 5, and the constant is 1.

importantKeywords.definition: A single part of an expression separated by + or -.
The number multiplying a variable in a term.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The coefficient of x in 4x is 4.

importantKeywords.definition: A term with no variable, a fixed number.

topics.quickSummary: An algebraic expression is built from terms; each term has a coefficient and a variable, while constant terms are just numbers.
revisionNotes: Terms are separated by + or - signs.
Coefficient is the number multiplying a variable.
A constant has no variable.
Expressions may use one or many variables.

topics.meaning: A univariate polynomial is an expression in one variable; its degree is the highest power, giving constant, linear, quadratic and cubic types.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Algebraic expressions involving only one variable and its powers are called univariate (one-variable) polynomials. The highest power of the variable is called the degree. A polynomial of degree 0 (a nonzero constant like 8 = 8x^0) is a constant polynomial; degree 1 (like 3z + 7) is a linear polynomial; degree 2 (like x^2 + 5x + 1) is a quadratic polynomial; and degree 3 (like 5y^3 + y^2 + 2y - 1) is a cubic polynomial. The coefficients are read off term by term; for example in 5y^3 + y^2 + 2y - 1 the coefficient of y^3 is 5 and the constant term is -1. This chapter focuses on linear polynomials, those of degree 1.
topics.easyExplanation: A polynomial is an expression using one letter raised to whole-number powers. The biggest power tells its degree: degree 1 is linear, 2 is quadratic, 3 is cubic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check it is one variable
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Confirm the expression uses a single variable and whole-number powers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the highest power
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The largest exponent of the variable is the degree.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name the type
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Degree 0 constant, 1 linear, 2 quadratic, 3 cubic.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read coefficients
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note the number multiplying each power and the constant term.

examples: 2x^2 - 5x + 3 has degree 2, so it is a quadratic polynomial.
4z - 3 has degree 1, so it is a linear polynomial.

importantKeywords.definition: An expression in one variable using whole-number powers.
The highest power of the variable in a polynomial.
A polynomial of degree 1.

topics.quickSummary: A univariate polynomial's degree is its highest power; degrees 0,1,2,3 give constant, linear, quadratic and cubic polynomials.
revisionNotes: Degree = highest power of the variable.
Degree 1 is linear, 2 quadratic, 3 cubic.
A nonzero constant has degree 0.
This chapter focuses on linear polynomials.

topics.meaning: A linear polynomial has degree 1; equating it to a constant gives a linear equation that can be solved for the variable.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A linear polynomial is a polynomial of degree 1, such as 4x (perimeter of a square of side x) or 200 + 50m (cost of m chess matches at fee 200 plus 50 per match). A key feature is that the difference between successive values at integers is constant. When we equate a linear polynomial in one variable to a constant, we obtain a linear equation. For example, if the sum of two numbers is 64 and one is 10 more than the other, then x + (x + 10) = 64 gives 2x + 10 = 64, so 2x = 54 and x = 27; the numbers are 27 and 37. Such equations model many real-life problems.
topics.easyExplanation: A linear polynomial is the simplest kind, like 50m + 200. If you set it equal to a number you get a linear equation, which you solve to find the unknown.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form the polynomial
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express the situation as a degree-1 expression in one variable.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Set up the equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Equate the polynomial to the given constant value.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine like terms to get the form ax + b = c.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve for x
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Isolate the variable to find its value.

examples: Chess cost 200 + 50m; if a player paid 750 then 200 + 50m = 750, so 50m = 550 and m = 11 matches.
Two numbers summing to 64 with one 10 more: 2x + 10 = 64 gives x = 27, numbers 27 and 37.

importantKeywords.definition: A degree-1 polynomial like ax + b.
An equation got by setting a linear polynomial equal to a constant.
The fixed change in value for each unit increase.

topics.quickSummary: A linear polynomial has degree 1 and constant successive differences; setting it equal to a constant gives a solvable linear equation.
revisionNotes: Linear polynomial has degree 1.
Successive integer values differ by a constant.
Equating to a constant gives a linear equation.
Solve ax + b = c by isolating x.

topics.topic: Linear Patterns and the nth Term
topics.meaning: A linear pattern is a sequence whose consecutive terms differ by a constant; its nth term is a linear expression in n.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A linear pattern is a sequence of numbers in which the difference between consecutive terms is constant. For a growing pattern of square tiles giving 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, ..., the number of tiles at stage n is 2n - 1, a linear polynomial of degree 1, and the constant difference is 2. Many situations follow linear patterns: Bela starting with 100 rupees and spending 5 daily has 100 - 5n left on day n; an auto fare of 25 for 2 km then 15 per km gives 15n - 5 for n at least 2. In each case the nth term is a linear expression in n, and substituting a value of n finds any term directly.
topics.easyExplanation: A linear pattern is a list of numbers that goes up or down by the same amount each step. You can write a simple rule using n to jump straight to any term.

stepByStepExplanation.title: List the terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Write out the first few terms of the pattern.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the common difference
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtract consecutive terms to confirm a constant difference.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the nth term
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express the rule as a linear expression like an + b.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute n
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put in the value of n to find any required term.

examples: Tile pattern 1,3,5,7,...: nth term = 2n - 1, so the 15th stage has 2(15) - 1 = 29 tiles.
Bela with 100 rupees spending 5 daily: amount left on day n = 100 - 5n, so on day 12 she has 100 - 60 = 40 rupees.

importantKeywords.definition: A sequence with a constant difference between consecutive terms.
A formula giving the term in position n.
The fixed amount added at each step.

topics.quickSummary: In a linear pattern terms differ by a constant; its nth term is a linear expression like an + b, letting us find any term directly.
revisionNotes: Consecutive terms differ by a constant.
The nth term is linear in n.
Tile pattern 1,3,5,7 has nth term 2n-1.
Substitute n to find any term.

topics.meaning: Linear growth is a pattern that increases by a constant amount over equal intervals; linear decay decreases by a constant amount.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Linear expressions model situations of steady growth or decline. Linear growth describes a quantity that increases by a fixed amount over equal intervals; for example the cost C(d) = 100 + 60d rises by 60 rupees per km. Linear decay describes a quantity that decreases by a fixed amount over equal intervals; for example the water height h(t) = 3 - 0.5t falls by 0.5 m per month. In both, the rate of change is constant. Growth corresponds to a positive coefficient of the variable, while decay corresponds to a negative coefficient.
topics.easyExplanation: If something goes up by the same amount every time, that's linear growth (like a salary rising steadily). If it drops by the same amount each time, that's linear decay (like water draining at a fixed rate).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the starting value
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Read the value when the variable is 0; this is the constant term.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the fixed change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Determine how much it rises or falls per unit.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Decide growth or decay
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Increase by a constant is growth; decrease is decay.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the expression
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Combine as start + (rate) x variable.

examples: Cost C(d) = 100 + 60d: at d = 0 cost is 100, rising 60 per km, so it is linear growth; for 15 km cost is 100 + 900 = 1000.
Height h(t) = 3 - 0.5t: starts at 3 m and falls 0.5 m per month, so it is linear decay; at t = 5, h = 3 - 2.5 = 0.5 m.

importantKeywords.definition: A steady increase by a constant amount over equal intervals.
A steady decrease by a constant amount over equal intervals.
The constant amount by which a quantity changes per unit.

topics.quickSummary: Linear growth rises by a constant amount each interval (positive rate) and linear decay falls by a constant amount (negative rate).
revisionNotes: Growth increases by a constant amount per interval.
Decay decreases by a constant amount per interval.
Growth has positive rate, decay negative.
Both are modelled by linear expressions.

topics.meaning: A linear relationship between two variables can be written y = ax + b and found from two known data pairs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A linear relationship between two variables x and y can be expressed as y = ax + b. For the tile pattern, with x the term number and y the number of tiles, y = 2x - 1. When two data pairs are known we can find a and b by substitution. For example, a telecom bill is 350 for 10 GB and 550 for 20 GB; substituting into y = ax + b gives 350 = 10a + b and 550 = 20a + b. Subtracting, 10a = 200 so a = 20, and then b = 150, giving y = 20x + 150. Here a is the cost per GB and b is the fixed monthly fee.
topics.easyExplanation: Many real situations link two quantities with a rule y = ax + b: a is how fast y changes, b is the starting amount. With two known points you can find a and b by solving simple equations.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write the form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Assume the relationship is y = ax + b.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute the data
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put each known (x, y) pair into the equation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve for a
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtract the two equations to eliminate b and find a.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve for b
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Substitute a back into one equation to find b.

examples: Bill 350 at 10 GB and 550 at 20 GB: 350=10a+b, 550=20a+b give a=20, b=150, so y=20x+150.
Celsius-Fahrenheit: using (0,32) and (100,212) one finds the linear relationship between the two scales.

importantKeywords.definition: A link between x and y of the form y = ax + b.
The rate at which y changes per unit increase in x.
The value of y when x is 0, the fixed amount.

topics.quickSummary: A linear relationship y = ax + b has slope a and intercept b, found from two known data pairs by substitution.
revisionNotes: Linear relationship has form y = ax + b.
a is the rate of change, b the starting value.
Use two data pairs to find a and b.
Subtract equations to eliminate b first.

topics.topic: Graphing Lines: Slope and the Role of a
topics.meaning: The graph of y = ax is a straight line through the origin; the value a is its slope, controlling steepness and direction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To plot a linear equation we find any two points on it, plot them and join with a straight line. Lines of the form y = ax always pass through the origin (0, 0). The number a is the slope of the line. When a > 1 the line is steeper than y = x; when a < 1 it is less steep. A positive slope (linear growth) rises from left to right, while a negative slope like y = -3x (linear decay) falls. For the tile relationship y = 2x - 1 the slope 2 equals the constant difference between consecutive terms of the sequence.
topics.easyExplanation: Drawing y = ax just needs two points; the line always goes through the centre. The number a tells how steep the line is, and whether it goes up (positive) or down (negative).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pick two points
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Choose two x-values and compute the matching y-values.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plot and join
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plot the points and draw a straight line through them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read the slope
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The coefficient a of x is the slope of the line.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Interpret a
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Large a is steep; positive a rises, negative a falls.

examples: For y = 2x take (0,0) and (1,2); the line through them has slope 2.
y = -3x has negative slope, so it falls steeply from left to right through the origin.

importantKeywords.definition: The number a measuring a line's steepness and direction.
How sharply a line rises or falls.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: y = 2x is steeper than y = x.

importantKeywords.definition: Passing through the point (0,0).
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Every line y = ax passes through (0,0).

topics.quickSummary: The graph of y = ax is a line through the origin whose slope a sets its steepness and direction.
revisionNotes: y = ax passes through the origin.
a is the slope of the line.
a > 1 steeper, a < 1 less steep than y = x.
Positive slope rises, negative slope falls.

topics.meaning: In y = ax + b, b is the y-intercept where the line crosses the y-axis; lines with equal slopes are parallel.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Any straight line written as y = ax + b cuts the y-axis at the point (0, b); the length b is called the y-intercept. For example y = x + 3 cuts the y-axis at (0, 3) and y = 3x - 2 cuts it at (0, -2). In y = ax + b, a is the slope and b the y-intercept. Changing a (keeping b fixed) changes the steepness but not where the line meets the y-axis. Changing b (keeping a fixed) shifts the line but keeps it parallel to the original; lines with equal slopes but different y-intercepts are parallel.
topics.easyExplanation: In y = ax + b, the number b tells where the line crosses the upright axis. If two lines have the same a (same tilt) they never meet, they are parallel.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify a and b
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Read the slope a and intercept b from y = ax + b.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the crossing point
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The line meets the y-axis at (0, b).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compare slopes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two lines with the same a have the same tilt.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Conclude parallelism
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Equal slopes with different b means the lines are parallel.

examples: y = x + 3 cuts the y-axis at (0,3); y = 3x - 2 cuts it at (0,-2).
y = 2x - 1, y = 2x + 1 and y = 2x + 5 all have slope 2, so they are parallel lines.

importantKeywords.definition: The value b where the line crosses the y-axis.
Lines that have the same slope and never meet.
Moving a line up or down by changing b.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Changing b from 1 to 5 shifts the line up.

topics.quickSummary: In y = ax + b the intercept b is where the line meets the y-axis; lines with equal slopes are parallel.
revisionNotes: Line y = ax + b meets the y-axis at (0,b).
a is slope, b is y-intercept.
Changing b shifts the line, keeping it parallel.
Equal slopes mean parallel lines.

topics.meaning: Natural numbers are the counting numbers; they arose from the ancient practice of matching one object to another.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Long before writing, humans needed to keep count. Without words or symbols for numbers, early herders used one-to-one correspondence: placing one pebble in a pot for each cow that left and removing one for each that returned, so an empty pot meant all were safe. This act of matching one object to another was the birth of the Natural Numbers, denoted N = {1, 2, 3, 4, ...}. The earliest physical records are tally marks on bone, such as the 35,000-year-old Lebombo Bone and the Ishango bone whose notches group the primes 11, 13, 17, 19. Natural numbers are closed under addition but not under subtraction.
topics.easyExplanation: Natural numbers are the everyday counting numbers 1, 2, 3 and so on. People first counted by matching things one-to-one, like one pebble for each cow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Match objects
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pair each item with one marker, such as a pebble or notch.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count by matching
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The collection of markers represents the quantity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Name the set
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: These counting numbers form N = {1, 2, 3, ...}.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Note closure
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Adding two naturals gives a natural, but subtracting may not.

examples: A herder places one pebble per cow leaving; if the pot is empty at night, no cow is missing.
5 + 4 = 9 is a natural number, but 3 - 5 is not a natural number, so naturals are not closed under subtraction.

importantKeywords.definition: The counting numbers N = {1, 2, 3, ...}.
Matching each object with exactly one marker.
A set is closed under an operation if the result stays in the set.

topics.quickSummary: Natural numbers N = {1,2,3,...} arose from one-to-one matching; they are closed under addition but not subtraction.
revisionNotes: Natural numbers are the counting numbers from 1.
They came from one-to-one correspondence.
Earliest records are tally marks on bone.
Closed under addition, not subtraction.

topics.meaning: Zero was transformed from a mere placeholder into a true number by Brahmagupta, who gave its arithmetic rules.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: For millennia the number line started at 1, and civilisations used placeholders without treating nothing as a number. Inspired by the philosophical idea of Shunyata (emptiness) from the Upanishads and Buddhist thought, Indian mathematicians welcomed nothingness as a concept. The Bakhshali Manuscript shows a bold dot (bindu) for zero. In his work Brahmasphutasiddhanta (628 CE), Brahmagupta defined zero as a - a = 0 and gave its rules: a + 0 = a, a - 0 = a, and a x 0 = 0. This transformed zero into a fully operational number, one of the most important inventions in mathematics.
topics.easyExplanation: Zero means nothing, but treating nothing as a real number you can add and multiply was a big idea. Brahmagupta gave the rules: adding zero changes nothing, and anything times zero is zero.

stepByStepExplanation.title: From placeholder to number
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Earlier cultures only used a gap or symbol for an empty column.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Philosophical roots
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The idea of emptiness (Shunyata) prepared the way for zero.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Brahmagupta defined zero as a - a = 0.

stepByStepExplanation.title: State the rules
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: a + 0 = a, a - 0 = a, and a x 0 = 0.

examples: a + 0 = a, for example 7 + 0 = 7.
a x 0 = 0, for example 25 x 0 = 0.

importantKeywords.definition: The number representing nothing, defined as a - a.
A symbol marking an empty column without being a number.
Laws like a + 0 = a and a x 0 = 0.

topics.quickSummary: Brahmagupta turned zero into a real number with rules a+0=a, a-0=a and a x 0 = 0.
revisionNotes: Zero was once only a placeholder.
Brahmagupta (628 CE) made it a true number.
a + 0 = a and a - 0 = a.
Any number times zero is zero.

topics.meaning: Integers are the natural numbers, their negatives, and zero; Brahmagupta introduced negatives as debts.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Brahmagupta asked what happens when a larger number is subtracted from a smaller one, such as 3 - 5. He grounded the answer in commerce, recognising two states: fortunes (dhana, positive) and debts (rina, negative). Moving left of zero on the number line gives the Negative Numbers. Together the positive naturals, their negatives, and zero form the Integers, denoted Z. Brahmagupta gave still-used rules: a fortune plus a fortune is a fortune; a debt plus a debt is a debt; the product of a debt and a fortune is a debt; and the product of two debts is a fortune, so (-3) x (-4) = 12.
topics.easyExplanation: Integers include the counting numbers, zero, and their negatives like -1, -2. Think of positives as money you have and negatives as money you owe.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Picture fortunes and debts
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Positive numbers are wealth, negatives are debt.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Go left of zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Negative integers sit to the left of zero on the number line.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Combine like signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fortune plus fortune is fortune; debt plus debt is debt.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Debt times fortune is debt; debt times debt is fortune.

examples: (-5) + (-4) = -9: owing 5 then borrowing 4 more means owing 9.
(-3) x (-4) = 12: removing four debts of 3 each leaves you 12 richer.

importantKeywords.definition: A number less than zero, representing a debt.
Two debts multiplied give a fortune.

topics.quickSummary: Integers Z combine naturals, zero and negatives; Brahmagupta's debt-and-fortune rules govern their arithmetic.
revisionNotes: Integers add negatives and zero to the naturals.
Positives are fortunes, negatives are debts.
Debt times fortune is debt.
Debt times debt is a fortune.

topics.meaning: A rational number is any number expressible as p/q with integers p and q and q not zero; they obey Brahmagupta's fraction rules.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Numbers representing parts of a whole are fractions; combining all integers and all positive and negative fractions gives the Rational Numbers, denoted Q. A rational number is any number that can be written as p/q where p and q are integers and q is not zero. Rationals are not uniquely represented, since equivalent fractions like 1/3 and 2/6 are equal; we usually use the form in lowest terms with co-prime p and q. Brahmagupta gave the rules: two rationals a/b and c/d are equal if ad = bc; addition uses a common denominator; multiplication is (a/b)(c/d) = ac/bd; and division multiplies by the reciprocal. Rationals are closed under addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division (except by zero).
topics.easyExplanation: A rational number is any fraction p/q where the bottom is not zero. This includes whole numbers and negatives too, and we add, multiply and divide them with the usual fraction rules.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Write as p/q
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Express the number as a fraction of two integers with q not zero.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use equivalent forms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reduce by cancelling common factors to lowest terms.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add with common denominator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Make denominators equal, then add or subtract numerators.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply and divide
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply across; divide by multiplying by the reciprocal.

importantKeywords.definition: Any number p/q with integers p, q and q not zero.
Different fractions representing the same value.
The fraction turned upside down, used in division.

topics.quickSummary: Rational numbers are all p/q with q not zero; they include integers and obey common fraction rules, closed under all four operations except division by zero.
revisionNotes: Rational number = p/q with q not zero.
Equivalent fractions represent the same value.
Add using a common denominator.
Closed under the four operations (not divide by 0).

topics.topic: Density and Number-Line Representation of Rationals
topics.meaning: Rational numbers are dense, meaning between any two there is always another, and they can be marked on the number line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To represent p/q on the number line, divide each unit interval into q equal parts and move p parts from 0, right if positive and left if negative; for example 3/4 is found by dividing 0 to 1 into four parts and moving three. The absolute value |x| is a number's distance from 0, always non-negative, and |a - b| is the distance between a and b. A remarkable property is density: between any two rationals there is always another, found by taking their average (a + b)/2. For instance, between 1 and 3/2 lies their average 5/4. This means there are infinitely many rationals between any two points.
topics.easyExplanation: You can mark any fraction on the number line by splitting each unit into equal parts. Amazingly, between any two fractions there is always another one, just take the middle (average) of the two.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Split the interval
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide the unit between integers into q equal parts.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Step out p parts
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Move p parts from 0, right if positive, left if negative.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Take averages
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The average (a+b)/2 always lies between a and b.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Repeat endlessly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: You can keep finding new rationals between any two, so they are dense.

examples: To mark 3/4, divide 0 to 1 into four parts and move three to the right.
A rational between 1 and 3/2 is (1 + 3/2)/2 = (5/2)/2 = 5/4.

importantKeywords.definition: The property that between any two rationals lies another rational.
The distance of a number from 0, written |x|.
The mean (a+b)/2 of two numbers.

topics.quickSummary: Rationals can be placed on the number line by splitting unit intervals, and they are dense because the average of any two lies between them.
revisionNotes: Mark p/q by splitting units into q parts and stepping p.
|x| is distance from 0; |a-b| is distance between a and b.
Average (a+b)/2 lies between a and b.
Infinitely many rationals lie between any two points.

topics.topic: Irrational Numbers and the Proof that sqrt(2) is Irrational
topics.meaning: Irrational numbers cannot be written as a ratio of integers; sqrt(2) is irrational, shown by proof by contradiction.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Numbers on the number line that cannot be expressed as a ratio of integers are called Irrational Numbers. The diagonal of a unit square has length sqrt(2) since d^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2, and Hippasus first proved sqrt(2) irrational using proof by contradiction. Assuming sqrt(2) = p/q in lowest terms (p, q co-prime), squaring gives 2q^2 = p^2, so p^2 is even, hence p is even, say p = 2k. Then 2q^2 = 4k^2 gives q^2 = 2k^2, so q is also even. But then p and q share the factor 2, contradicting that the fraction was in lowest terms. Hence sqrt(2) cannot be a fraction; it is irrational.
topics.easyExplanation: Some numbers, like the square root of 2, can never be written exactly as a fraction. We prove this by assuming it can, then showing this leads to a contradiction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Assume it is rational
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Suppose sqrt(2) = p/q in lowest terms with p, q co-prime.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Square both sides
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Squaring gives 2q^2 = p^2, so p^2 is even and p is even.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Substitute p = 2k
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Then 2q^2 = 4k^2 gives q^2 = 2k^2, so q is even too.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reach contradiction
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Both p and q are even, contradicting lowest terms, so sqrt(2) is irrational.

examples: The diagonal of a unit square is sqrt(1^2 + 1^2) = sqrt(2), an irrational length.
Assuming sqrt(2) = p/q leads to both p and q being even, a contradiction, proving sqrt(2) is irrational.

importantKeywords.definition: A number that cannot be written as a ratio of integers.
Assuming the opposite and showing it leads to a contradiction.
Two integers sharing no common factor other than 1.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: p and q in lowest terms.

topics.quickSummary: Irrational numbers cannot be written as p/q; sqrt(2) is irrational, proved by contradiction from the diagonal of a unit square.
revisionNotes: Irrationals are not ratios of integers.
sqrt(2) is the diagonal of a unit square.
Proof by contradiction assumes the opposite.
Both p and q even contradicts lowest terms.

topics.meaning: Pi, the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter, is irrational and is given exactly by Madhava's infinite series.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Pi is the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter and is another famous irrational number. Aryabhata gave the accurate approximation 3927/1250 = 3.1416 but called it only an asanna (approximation). Because pi is irrational (proved by Lambert in 1761), no single fraction can ever give it exactly. An exact formula was first found in the 14th century by Madhava of Sangamagrama, who realised that an infinite sum is needed: pi = 4 x (1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7 + ...). An infinite sum means the value we approach more and more closely as we add more terms. Pi has a non-terminating, non-repeating decimal expansion 3.14159....
topics.easyExplanation: Pi is the number you get by dividing any circle's distance around by its width across. It can never be a neat fraction, but Madhava found an endless adding pattern that gives it exactly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Define pi
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pi is circumference divided by diameter, the same for every circle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Try approximations
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fractions like 3927/1250 only approximate pi.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recognise irrationality
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: No finite fraction equals pi exactly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use the infinite series
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Madhava's pi = 4(1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7 + ...) gives it exactly.

examples: Aryabhata's approximation 3927/1250 = 3.1416 is close but not exact.
Madhava's series: pi = 4 x (1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7 + ...).

importantKeywords.definition: The constant ratio of circumference to diameter of a circle.
An endless sum whose value is approached term by term.
A close but not exact value.

topics.quickSummary: Pi is the irrational circumference-to-diameter ratio; Madhava gave the exact infinite series pi = 4(1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - ...).
revisionNotes: Pi = circumference divided by diameter.
Pi is irrational, so never an exact fraction.
Madhava found the exact infinite series.
Pi's decimal never ends or repeats.

topics.meaning: Rational numbers have decimals that either terminate or repeat; the type can be predicted from the prime factors of the denominator.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Uniting rationals and irrationals gives the Real Numbers R, an unbroken line. The easiest way to tell a rational from an irrational is its decimal expansion: a rational either terminates (like 3/8 = 0.375) or repeats (like 5/11 = 0.4545...), while an irrational never ends and never repeats. A rational p/q in lowest terms has a terminating decimal precisely when the prime factors of q are only 2, only 5, or both, because then the denominator can be made a power of 10. Repeating decimals occur because, in long division, only finitely many remainders are possible, so a remainder must eventually repeat, looping the digits.
topics.easyExplanation: If you turn a fraction into a decimal, it either stops or repeats a block forever. You can predict which by looking at whether the bottom has only 2s and 5s as prime factors.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reduce to lowest terms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Cancel common factors so p and q are co-prime.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Factor the denominator
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find the prime factors of q.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Predict the type
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Only 2s and 5s means terminating; otherwise repeating.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Check by division
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide to see the decimal terminate or loop.

examples: 3/20 has denominator 20 = 2^2 x 5, only 2s and 5s, so it terminates: 3/20 = 0.15.
5/11 repeats because 11 is not 2 or 5: 5/11 = 0.4545... = 0.45 repeating.

importantKeywords.definition: A decimal that ends after finitely many digits.
A decimal with a block of digits that loops forever.
All rationals and irrationals together, forming R.

topics.quickSummary: Rationals have terminating or repeating decimals; a terminating decimal occurs exactly when the denominator's prime factors are only 2 and 5.
revisionNotes: Rationals terminate or repeat; irrationals never repeat.
Only 2s and 5s in the denominator means terminating.
Repeating happens because remainders must repeat.
Reals R unite rationals and irrationals.

topics.meaning: A repeating decimal can be converted to p/q by setting it equal to x, multiplying by powers of 10, and subtracting.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Repeating decimals can be written as fractions using an algebraic technique. For a pure repeating decimal like 0.6 repeating, let x = 0.6..., multiply by 10 (since one digit repeats) to get 10x = 6.6..., subtract to get 9x = 6, so x = 6/9 = 2/3. For two repeating digits multiply by 100, and so on. For a general repeating decimal with non-repeating digits first, multiply by 10^m to move the non-repeating digits before the decimal, then by a further power of 10 to move one full repeating cycle, and subtract; for 0.16 repeating (1 non-repeating, 6 repeating) this gives x = 15/90 = 1/6. Also, any terminating decimal has an equivalent form with repeating 9s, so 0.999... = 1.
topics.easyExplanation: To turn a repeating decimal into a fraction, call it x, multiply by 10 or 100 so the repeating parts line up, then subtract to cancel the endless tail and solve for x.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Let x equal the decimal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Set x equal to the repeating decimal.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply by a power of 10
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply so the repeating block shifts left by one full cycle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Subtract the equations
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtracting cancels the infinite repeating tail.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Solve for x
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide to get x as a fraction and simplify.

examples: 0.6 repeating: 10x = 6.6..., subtract x = 0.6... gives 9x = 6, so x = 2/3.
0.45 repeating: 100x = 45.45..., subtract x gives 99x = 45, so x = 45/99 = 5/11.

importantKeywords.definition: A decimal that repeats immediately after the point.
A decimal with non-repeating digits before the repeating block.
A terminating decimal equals one with repeating 9s.

topics.quickSummary: Repeating decimals convert to fractions by letting x equal them, multiplying by powers of 10, and subtracting to remove the tail.
revisionNotes: Let x equal the repeating decimal.
Multiply by 10^n to shift one repeating cycle.
Subtract to cancel the infinite tail.
0.999... equals exactly 1.

topics.meaning: An algebraic identity is true for all values of its variables, whereas an ordinary equation is true only for some values.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic identity is an equation that is true for all values of the variables occurring in it, while an ordinary equation need not be. For example, x^2 - 1 = 24 is true only for x = 5 or x = -5, so it is an equation. But (x + y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2 is true for every value of x and y, so it is an identity. Identities are special rules that simplify complicated calculations and let us work efficiently with algebraic expressions. They can be verified for numbers, including negatives and rationals, and proved in general using the distributive property.
topics.easyExplanation: An identity is a math statement that works no matter what numbers you plug in. An ordinary equation is only true for certain special numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recall an equation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An equation like x^2 - 1 = 24 holds only for special x values.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recall an identity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: An identity like (x+y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2 holds for all values.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Test with numbers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Substitute several values to see an identity always works.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Prove generally
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use the distributive property to confirm it for all values.

examples: x^2 - 1 = 24 is true only when x = 5 or x = -5, so it is an equation.
(x + y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2 is true for every x and y, so it is an identity.

importantKeywords.definition: An equation true for all values of its variables.
A statement true only for particular values.
a(b + c) = ab + ac, used to prove identities.

topics.quickSummary: An identity holds for all values of its variables, unlike an ordinary equation which holds only for some.
revisionNotes: Identities are true for all values.
Equations are true only for some values.
Test identities by substitution.
Prove them with the distributive property.

topics.meaning: The square of a sum equals the first square plus twice the product plus the second square, shown geometrically by area.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The identity (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 can be visualised by partitioning a square of side (a + b) into a square of area a^2, a square of area b^2, and two rectangles each of area ab. Together these make the whole square, giving the identity. It holds for all numbers including negatives and rationals, and is proved generally by (a + b)(a + b) = a(a+b) + b(a+b) = a^2 + 2ab + b^2. It can be used to expand binomials and to speed up calculations such as squaring numbers near round values.
topics.easyExplanation: Squaring a sum is not just squaring each part. There is an extra middle piece: (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2. A square cut into pieces shows why.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Draw the big square
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Draw a square of side (a + b).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cut into pieces
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It splits into squares a^2 and b^2 and two rectangles ab.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Add the areas
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: a^2 + b^2 + 2ab equals the whole square (a+b)^2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use it to expand binomials or square numbers quickly.

importantKeywords.definition: An expression with two terms.
The middle term 2ab in the expansion.

topics.quickSummary: (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2, with the middle 2ab term shown by two rectangles in the area diagram.
revisionNotes: A square of side a+b shows the four pieces.
Do not forget the middle 2ab term.
Use it to expand binomials and square numbers.

topics.meaning: The square of a difference equals the first square minus twice the product plus the second square.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Replacing b by -b in (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 gives the identity (a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2, which is also valid for all values. It can be visualised by starting from a square of side a, marking off a length b, and subtracting the rectangle areas to leave the small square of side (a - b). Like the sum identity, it is used both to factor expressions and to compute squares of numbers just below round values quickly.
topics.easyExplanation: Squaring a difference is similar but the middle piece is subtracted: (a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2. It is handy for squaring numbers like 29 (just below 30).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Replace b by -b
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Substitute -b into the sum identity.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The middle term becomes negative: -2ab.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use it to compute squares or to factor expressions.

importantKeywords.definition: The result of subtracting one quantity from another.
An expression equal to a squared binomial.

topics.quickSummary: (a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2, obtained by replacing b with -b in the sum identity.
revisionNotes: Got by replacing b with -b.
Middle term is subtracted.
Use for squaring numbers just below round values.

topics.topic: The Difference of Squares and Three-Term Square
topics.meaning: a^2 - b^2 factors as (a + b)(a - b), and (a + b + c)^2 expands into the squares and pairwise products.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The difference of two squares factors as a^2 - b^2 = (a + b)(a - b), which can be rewritten as a^2 = (a + b)(a - b) + b^2, a method Shridharacharya used to square numbers quickly (e.g. 55^2 = 60 x 50 + 25 = 3025). The square of three terms is (a + b + c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2ab + 2bc + 2ca, derived by replacing d with (b + c) in (a + d)^2, and visualised by a square of side (a + b + c) split into three smaller squares and six rectangles. These identities help with quick squaring and with factoring.
topics.easyExplanation: Two squares subtracted factor neatly as (a+b)(a-b). And squaring a sum of three terms gives each squared plus twice every pair multiplied.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Difference of squares
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: a^2 - b^2 splits into (a + b)(a - b).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Quick squaring
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use a^2 = (a+b)(a-b) + b^2 to square numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Apply
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Use these to factor expressions or square numbers fast.

importantKeywords.definition: The cross terms 2ab, 2bc, 2ca.

revisionNotes: a^2 - b^2 factors as (a+b)(a-b).
a^2 = (a+b)(a-b) + b^2 squares numbers quickly.
(a+b+c)^2 adds three squares and six cross terms.
Both help with factoring and fast squaring.

topics.meaning: Algebra tiles model a product like (x + 3)(x + 4) as a rectangle, revealing the factors of x^2 + 7x + 12.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A rectangle with sides (x + 3) and (x + 4) has area (x + 3)(x + 4) = x^2 + 7x + 12. Using algebra tiles, x + 3 is shown by an x-tile and three unit tiles and x + 4 by an x-tile and four unit tiles; the product is the inner rectangle made of one x^2-tile, seven x-tiles, and twelve unit tiles. Here 7x is split as 3x + 4x and the 12 units form a 3 by 4 array. The arrangement shows both directions of the identity: multiplying the linear factors gives x^2 + 7x + 12, and factoring x^2 + 7x + 12 gives (x + 3)(x + 4). Generally (x + a)(x + b) = x^2 + (a + b)x + ab.
topics.easyExplanation: Algebra tiles are squares and strips you arrange into a rectangle. The sides of the rectangle are the factors, so building x^2 + 7x + 12 into a neat rectangle shows it equals (x + 3)(x + 4).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Lay out the factors
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Represent each factor with an x-tile and unit tiles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Form the rectangle
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Arrange tiles into a rectangle with those sides.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Count the tiles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Count x^2-tiles, x-tiles and unit tiles inside.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Read the factors
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The rectangle's sides give the factors.

examples: (x + 3)(x + 4) = x^2 + 7x + 12, with 7x split as 3x + 4x.
(x + a)(x + b) = x^2 + (a + b)x + ab.

importantKeywords.definition: Square and strip pieces modelling algebraic terms.
An expression that divides another exactly.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: x + 3 is a factor of x^2 + 7x + 12.

importantKeywords.definition: A rectangular arrangement of unit tiles.

topics.quickSummary: Algebra tiles arrange a product into a rectangle whose sides are the factors, modelling (x+a)(x+b) = x^2 + (a+b)x + ab.
revisionNotes: Tiles form a rectangle whose sides are the factors.
7x is split as 3x + 4x.
General rule: (x+a)(x+b) = x^2 + (a+b)x + ab.

topics.topic: Factorising by Splitting the Middle Term
topics.meaning: A quadratic x^2 + (a+b)x + ab is factored by finding two numbers whose sum is the x-coefficient and product is the constant.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To factor x^2 + 7x + 12 without tiles, we write it as x^2 + (a + b)x + ab and find a and b such that a + b equals the coefficient of x and ab equals the constant term. Here a + b = 7 and ab = 12 give a = 3 and b = 4, so x^2 + 7x + 12 = (x + 3)(x + 4). The same splitting works when constants are negative: for x^2 - 7x + 12 we need a + b = -7 and ab = 12, giving -3 and -4. This middle-term splitting also factors quadratics arising in real problems, such as finding the dimensions of a rectangle or pool of given area.
topics.easyExplanation: To factor a quadratic, find two numbers that add to the middle number and multiply to the last number. Those two numbers give the factors.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify the targets
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Note the coefficient of x (the sum) and the constant (the product).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the pair
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Find two numbers adding to the sum and multiplying to the product.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Split the middle term
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rewrite the x-term using those two numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Group and factor
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Group terms and take common factors to get the two factors.

examples: x^2 + 8x + 15: need sum 8, product 15, so 3 and 5; thus (x + 3)(x + 5).
x^2 - 4x - 96 = 0: -12 and 8 give -4x and -96, so (x - 12)(x + 8) = 0, giving x = 12.

importantKeywords.definition: Writing the x-term as a sum to enable factoring.
A degree-2 expression like x^2 + bx + c.
Two numbers with a given sum and product.

topics.quickSummary: A quadratic factors by finding two numbers whose sum is the x-coefficient and product is the constant, then splitting the middle term.
revisionNotes: Find two numbers adding to the middle, multiplying to the last.
Split the middle term using them.
Group and take common factors.
